title: Demonetized YouTube Channels - the Inauthentic Content Trap

transcript.text: In authentic content. Yep, it's still here along with its siblings, reuse and repetitious content and they're still causing huge headaches for creators. So in this video, what I want to achieve is to show you three main triggers that are causing this demonetization wave that seems endless right now. We'll investigate some of the channels that have been demonetized due to inauthentic content and how to steer clear of it. So that by the end of this video, you'll have a little more knowledge and hopefully a lot more confidence in how to protect your own channel from all of it. Channels are getting wiped out of monetization right now doing things you might be doing today. And from all the videos I've seen recently and you've probably seen the same ones on this particular subject, the majority of creators simply have no idea why this is happening. Now, let me be clear about something before we go any further down this path. YouTube's monetization rules on their support page are mid at best. Some might say intentionally vague, but likely for legal reasons, YouTube needs some vagueness to give it flexibility when applying these policies. I think that is why so many creators are getting demonetized doing stuff they don't feel is wrong. They're often not breaking any clearly written rules. Instead, they're being caught by something else mentioned on a completely different support page that maybe has nothing to do with monetization. Or they're failing the silent test, the advertiser test. They need confidence in the content they're running ads against. And if they don't have that confidence, well, that's bad news for every single monetized channel on the platform. With all of that being said, let's leap flamboyantly into trap number one. You built your channel on something you don't own. It could be a video game, a movie, a TV character, a celebrity's face. There are hundreds, maybe thousands of channels just like this on YouTube already. But if the answer is no, you're kind of just stuck decorating someone else's living room and selling tickets at the door to take part in a quiz. Now, even if you do nail transformation perfectly, here's the part that should really sober you up. There's YouTube who decides if you stay in the YouTube Partner Program, and then there's a copyright owner who can come for your revenue at any time they fancy with no warning needed, and you'll have a big fight on your hands to prove said transformation or fair use. And in very extreme circumstances, there's copyright law itself, which only gets fully decided in a courtroom you never want to find yourself in. So, if you do want long-term passive sleep at night monetization, build on your own characters, your own brand, your own world. Right then, trap number two. And this one stings even more because creators who fall into this are actually working their creative backsides off. On March 24th, without any warning, YouTube demonetized my channel for mass-produced repetitive content. Let me show you what mass-produced actually means in my case. I make every video by myself. Don't rely on an AI voice, and add more evidence that proves a human is working on the channel, even if you don't want to show your face.
